perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of confidentiality as it keeps its own confidential information, but in all events no less than a reaso nable degree of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information with a similar degree of confidentiality as it treats its other confidential information, but a minimal level of confidentiality will be maintained.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear, though somewhat subjective, standard of 'no less than a reasonable degree of confidentiality.' The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'a similar degree' and 'a minimal level,' making it unclear what level of protection is actually required. This weakening of the confidentiality requirement creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of trade secret protection laws. 18 U.S. Code § 1836 aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. By setting a 'minimal' level of confidentiality, the agreement risks failing to provide adequate protection, which could be construed as enabling misappropriation. 'Minimal level' introduces unacceptable ambiguity, making it harder to prove if the receiving party upheld its obligation to protect the information. What one party considers 'minimal' might be inadequate for safeguarding trade secrets. This makes enforcement difficult and could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent the intention of trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text uses "a similar degree" and "a minimal level" of confidentiality, which weakens the original requirement of "no less than a reasonable degree of confidentiality." The Law Explanation states that setting a 'minimal' level of confidentiality risks failing to provide adequate protection and introduces unacceptable ambiguity. This makes enforcement difficult and could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent the intention of trade secret law. While scraped snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) mainly discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, the Law Explanation suggests that weakening the confidentiality requirement could enable misappropriation, thereby contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets. The ambiguity in the modified text undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if so required by judicial or administrative proces s, provided that it shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party so as to allow the Disclosing Part y reasonable time to oppose such process, and provided that Receiving Party shall minimize su ch disclosure to the extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by judicial or administrative process, and will attempt to notify the Disclosing Party when feasible, and will try to minimize disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation to 'promptly notify' the Disclosing Party and 'minimize such disclosure to the extent possible.' The modified text weakens these obligations by using 'attempt to notify when feasible' and 'try to minimize disclosure,' introducing ambiguity. 'When feasible' and 'try' are subjective and weaken the contractual obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3) provides mechanisms for protecting those subject to subpoenas, including quashing or modifying a subpoena if it requires disclosing privileged or protected matter. While the modified NDA clause states the receiving party 'will try to minimize disclosure,' this is insufficient. It contradicts the responsibility the receiving party has to make a 'reasonable effort' to protect trade secrets. The modification weakens the duty to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with legal standards for protecting privileged information during discovery, which often require more than a mere 'attempt.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that weakens the original contractual obligation to protect confidential information. The original text had a clear obligation to 'promptly notify' and 'minimize such disclosure to the extent possible,' while the modified text introduces subjective terms like 'when feasible' and 'try', which create ambiguity. While the snippets describe the general rules of subpoena, the Law Explanation argues that the weakened language contradicts the responsibility to make a 'reasonable effort' to protect trade secrets, which implies a higher standard than merely 'trying'. The modification does not ensure a 'reasonable effort' and thus poses a legal contradiction by not sufficiently protecting privileged information during discovery, as the original text implies.

perturbation.original_text: Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party together with certification that any other copies or notes or summaries (to the extent i ncluding the Confidential Information) have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return most of the Confidential Information in tangible form, including easily accessible copies, to the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party will also provide an attestation that, to the best of their understanding, all other copies, notes, or summaries have been addressed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a full return of *all* confidential information and certification of destruction of *any* other copies. The modified text introduces ambiguity with 'most,' 'easily accessible copies,' and 'to the best of their understanding.' This creates uncertainty about the completeness of the return and the thoroughness of the destruction, making it difficult to enforce complete compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets. The ambiguous changes to the return of information at the end of the NDA potentially conflict with the DTSA by not ensuring complete return and destruction of confidential information. The phrases 'most of the confidential information,' 'easily accessible copies,' and 'to the best of their understanding' provide loopholes where the receiving party might not return or destroy all confidential information, which could leave the disclosing party vulnerable to trade secret misappropriation. These phrases weaken the protection of trade secrets provided under federal law.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity around the return of confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. Specifically, the original text mandates the return of *all* confidential information and certification of destruction of *any* other copies. The changed text uses terms like 'most of the Confidential Information,' 'easily accessible copies,' and 'to the best of their understanding,' which weakens the obligation to ensure a complete return and destruction of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, implying a legal duty to protect such secrets. The ambiguous language in the modified text could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect trade secrets, as it creates loopholes for the receiving party to avoid full compliance. This ambiguity directly impacts the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement and introduces a potential contradiction with the intent of trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall assign or otherwise transfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third pa rty without the prior written consent of the other party
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will attempt to assign its rights or obligations under this Agreement to a third party without first discussing it with the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text forbids assignment without 'prior written consent'. The modification introduces several ambiguities. 'Will attempt' implies that assignment might occur regardless of consent. Replacing 'written consent' with 'discussing it' removes the mandatory requirement for approval. This creates uncertainty and weakens the original prohibition.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, the modification goes against established principles of contract law regarding assignment. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 317 et seq. addresses assignment. An express clause forbidding assignment (as in the original) is generally enforceable. By changing 'shall not assign' to 'will attempt to assign... without discussing,' the clause creates ambiguity and potential grounds for arguing that assignment is permissible even without formal consent, undermining the original intent of the agreement and established contract law regarding restrictions on assignment. The modification does not explicitly prevent the assignment but makes the assignment only needing to be discussed, therefore, it contradicts the rule that the original party should not assign.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: assignment | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you assignment Assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another, known as the “ assignee .” This concept is used in both contract and property law. The term can refer to either the act of transfer or the rights /property/benefits being transferred. Contract Law Under contract law, assignment of a contract is both an assignment of rights and a delegation of duties in the absence of evidence otherwise. For example, if A contracts with B to teach B guitar for $50, A can assign this contract to C. That is, this assignment is both an assignment of A’s rights under the contract to the $50; and a delegation of A’s duty to teach guitar to C. In this example, A is both the “assignor” and the “delegee” who delegates the duties to another (C), C is known as the “ obligor ” who must perform the obligations to the assignee, and B is the “assignee” who is owed duties and is liable to the “obligor.” Assignment of Rights/Duties Under Contract Law There are a few notable rules regarding assignments under contract law. First, if an individual has not yet secured the contract to perform duties to another, he/she cannot assign his/her future right to an assignee. That is, if A has not yet contracted with B to teach B guitar, A cannot assign his/her rights to C. Second, rights cannot be assigned when they materially change the obligor’s duty and rights. Third, the obligor can sue the assignee directly if the assignee does not pay them. Following the previous example, this means that C (obligor) can sue B (assignee) if C teaches guitar to B, but B does not pay C $50 in return. Delegation of Duties If the promised performance requires a rare genius or skill, then the delegee cannot delegate it to the obligor. It can only be delegated if the promised performance is more commonplace. Further, an obligee can sue if the assignee does not perform. However, the delegee is secondarily liable unless there has been an express release of the delegee. That is, if B does want C to teach guitar but C refuses to, then B can sue C. If C still refuses to perform, then B can compel A to fulfill the duties under secondary liability. Lastly, a related concept is novation , which is when a new obligor substitutes and releases an old obligor. If novation occurs, then the original obligor’s duties are wiped out. However, novation requires an original obligee’s consent . Property Law Under property law, assignment typically arises in landlord-tenant situations. For example, A might be renting from landlord B but wants another party (C) to take over the property. In this scenario, A might be able to choose between assigning and subleasing the property to C. If assigning, A would be giving the entire balance of the term to C, with no reversion to anyone; whereas if subleasing, A would be giving the balance to C for a limited period of the remaining term. Under assignment, C would have privity of estate with the landlord, while under a sublease C would not. [Last reviewed in January of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law trusts inheritances & estates business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly forbids assignment without prior written consent. The modified text replaces this prohibition with a requirement to merely 'discuss' the assignment, creating ambiguity and weakening the restriction. According to the provided Wex definition, 'assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another.' The original clause prevents this transfer without written consent, while the changed clause only requires discussion, implicitly permitting assignment after discussion regardless of consent. This contradicts the original intent to prevent assignment and undermines the enforceability of the restriction. The modification doesn't explicitly prevent the assignment, but makes it conditional only on discussion. Therefore, it can be interpreted as allowing the assignment without consent.
